text: This is a laparoscopic removal of inguinal hernia mesh placed during a robotic tap hernia repair in a patient with chronic pain. An eighteen-year-old healthy male presented to an outside hospital with acute onset of left lower quadrant abdominal pain. Extensive workup including CT and lab work did not reveal a source. On CT scan, an incidental small left inguinal hernia was found and he underwent an expertly done robotic tap inguinal hernia repair with mesh.
Postoperatively, the pain continued to worsen. He developed systemic symptoms as well as psychological distress. Another extensive workup failed to reveal the cause of these symptoms.

The patient presented to our office requesting mesh removal after three months of worsening symptoms. We had a thorough discussion of the risks of mesh removal, including ongoing groin pain and systemic symptoms, bleeding, infection, numbness, testicular pain or chitis or ischemia, visceral and vascular injury, recurrent or new hernia formation, and bowel obstruction.
The laparoscopic view shows the patient's abdomen with the feet oriented at twelve o'clock. A marked area indicating the patient's reported pain is visible on the skin surface.

The surgeon identifies an inferior folded edge of the mesh during the laparoscopic examination. The abdominal wall is inspected, revealing no scar tissue or spigellian hernia. The posterior rectus fascia is explored, showing no visible pathology such as neuroma or intramuscular lipoma.
The surgeon begins the mesh excision by extending the peritoneal incision laterally and inferiorly, creating a flap that exposes the inguinal region and the underlying mesh.
The surgeon maintains hemostasis using monopolar cautery, ensuring clear visualization of the anatomical planes and structures during the procedure.
The surgeon exposes the inferior edge of the mesh with a second peritoneal incision. The incisions are connected to complete the flap, and the dissection of the mesh is carried medially.
The surgeon employs sharp dissection with electrocautery to carefully separate tissue layers, using a combination of techniques to navigate the surgical field.
The surgeon mobilizes the inferior clam-shelled edge of the mesh from the myobactinial orifice, revealing a cord lipoma. The lipoma is excised as a potential source of the patient's pain.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens and cord structures, carefully mobilizing them free from the surrounding mesh using sharp dissection techniques.
